justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Auth per consumer type correctly designed — JWT for mobile, API key for partners, with separate listeners. (2) Internal traffic separation fully addressed — separate port 8080 on private subnet, no auth, network-level isolation enforced. (3) Business logic explicitly excluded from gateway in multiple places including dedicated pitfall callout. (4) SPOF risk identified as Critical severity with detailed mitigation (3 instances, health checks, circuit breakers, fail-open/closed policies). (5) Webhook delivery for partners addressed with dedicated async Webhook Dispatcher, exponential backoff, DLQ, and circuit breaker per partner endpoint. (6) Both flexibility_points and rigidity_points present with detailed tables. (7) Technology recommendation includes build/buy consideration — recommends Kong for self-hosted, AWS API Gateway for cloud-native, explicitly warns against building custom.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough component table with clear responsibility boundaries — each component explicitly states what it does NOT own
Strangler fig migration pattern is a strong addition — addresses real-world cutover risk that the rubric didn't explicitly require
Rate limiting design is sophisticated — sliding window via Redis Lua script, keyed by authenticated identity not IP, with explicit callout of carrier NAT problem
Webhook delivery design is production-grade — async queue, exponential backoff with cap, dead letter queue, circuit breaker per partner, partner dashboard for visibility

Internal traffic separation goes beyond 'mentioned' — dedicated listener, separate port, private subnet binding, network policy enforcement, and explicitly marked as intentionally rigid security invariant
Build vs buy clearly addressed with concrete product recommendations (Kong, Envoy, AWS API Gateway) mapped to team context (self-hosted vs cloud-native)
Rigidity points section doesn't fully match the expected format — missing the specific rigidity about URL-path-based routing requiring gateway config changes when service endpoints are renamed
Pitfalls section is unusually practical — rate limiting by identity not IP, don't share counters across consumer types, don't skip strangler fig

agent_output_excerpt:
## Executive Summary

The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer that consolidates authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation — currently scattered inconsistently across individual services. I recommend a **single logical gateway with three distinct listener configurations** (mobile, partner, internal), backed by a plugin/middleware pipeline architecture. This eliminates auth inconsistency, centralizes policy enforcement, and creates a clean boundary between external cons
